front: What is the Axiom of Choice?
back: The Axiom of Choice states that given any collection of non-empty sets, it is possible to choose exactly one element from each set in the collection, even if the collection is infinite. Formally: For every indexed family {S_i}_{i ∈ I} of nonempty sets, there exists an indexed family {x_i}_{i ∈ I} of elements such that x_i ∈ S_i for every i ∈ I.

Zorn's Lemma states that if every chain (a totally ordered subset) in a partially ordered set (P, ≤) has an upper bound in P, then P contains at least one maximal element. It is equivalent to the Axiom of Choice.

front: Define a chain (or totally ordered set) in the context of partial orders.
back: In a partially ordered set (P, ≤), a chain is a subset C of P such that for every two elements a, b in C, either a ≤ b or b ≤ a. This means every pair of elements in C is comparable, making C totally ordered by the restriction of ≤.

front: What is a maximal element in a partially ordered set?
back: An element m in a partially ordered set (P, ≤) is maximal if there is no other element x in P such that m < x (i.e., m ≤ x and m ≠ x). Note: A set can have more than one maximal element.

front: How are the Axiom of Choice, Zorn's Lemma, and Well-Ordering Principle related?
back: The Axiom of Choice, Zorn's Lemma, and the Well-Ordering Principle are all logically equivalent statements within Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory. This means that assuming one of them allows you to prove the others. They are fundamental tools for handling existence proofs in infinite sets.
